summary: Cleanup PR for the fighter core around fighter.c, mainly replacing anonymous func_8006xxxx names with Fighter-prefixed symbols, improving local variable/type clarity, and synchronizing declarations and remaining assembly callsites. The PR touched fighter.c/fighter.h, include/functions.h, several Master Hand files, Koopa/player C files, and assembly for ft code, Ice Climbers, and Crazy Hand. No reviewer comments were present, so intent is inferred from the title and diff.

key_files.reason: Primary cleanup target; renamed many func_8006xxxx routines, clarified locals/types, adjusted callback registrations, and used Fighter fields/enums more explicitly.
Updated fighter function prototypes to the new Fighter_* names and improved parameter typing/names.
Kept global declarations/comments aligned with renamed fighter functions.
Updated remaining assembly branch/callback symbol references from func_8006xxxx to new Fighter_* names.
Updated calls to func_8006CFBC to Fighter_UnkSetFlag_8006CFBC in Ice Climbers assembly.
Updated calls to func_8006CFBC to Fighter_UnkSetFlag_8006CFBC in Crazy Hand assembly.
Multiple Master Hand files were updated for renamed fighter helper references.
Updated Koopa fighter references to renamed fighter helpers.

what_changed: Renamed many fighter.c functions from generic func_8006xxxx names to Fighter-prefixed names while preserving address suffixes, e.g. func_8006C0F0 -> Fighter_UnkApplyTransformation_8006C0F0, func_8006CFBC -> Fighter_UnkSetFlag_8006CFBC, and func_8006D044 -> Fighter_UnkRecursiveFunc_8006D044.

Renamed damage-related wrapper func_8006CC30 to Fighter_UnkTakeDamage_8006CC30 and changed its prototype from void func_8006CC30(f32 arg0, void* unk_ptr) to void Fighter_UnkTakeDamage_8006CC30(Fighter* fighter, f32 arg0) in fighter.c, with fighter.h exposing the damage_amount argument name.

Updated callback registration calls through func_8038FD54 to use renamed functions such as Fighter_8006C27C, Fighter_8006C5F4, Fighter_CallAcessoryCallbacks_8006C624, Fighter_UnkProcessGrab_8006CA5C, Fighter_UnkProcessShieldHit_8006D1EC, and Fighter_UnkCallCameraCallback_8006D9EC.

Synchronized assembly callsites and function pointer loads in asm/melee/ft/code_800877F8.s, fticeclimber.s, and ftcrazyhand.s so branch targets and @ha/@l address references point at renamed Fighter_* symbols.

Cleaned fighter.c locals by replacing misleading names such as jobj_userdata, fighter_r31, temp_ptr, spB0, mtx1, and mtx2 with more meaningful names like fighter, cam_offset, jobj_mtx, and fighter_mtx where the diff shows the usage was understood.
Replaced several explicit comparisons with idiomatic boolean checks, e.g. flag == 0 became !flag and pointer != 0U became direct pointer truth checks.
Used known enum names for ground/air checks in some locations, changing numeric comparisons like xE0_ground_or_air == 0 or == 1 to GA_Ground and GA_Air.

Updated struct field usage where the underlying type was clarified, e.g. fighterObj->user_data was treated as Fighter* rather than HSD_JObj*, and accesses changed from translate.y/mtx to Fighter fields such as x34_scale.z and x44_mtx.

Kept one unresolved external-style prototype as func_8006DABC(HSD_GObj* fighterObj) with a comment that it is currently in ftanim.s, showing the cleanup did not rename symbols without enough ownership/evidence.
smart_moves: Preserved original address suffixes in every new name, making renamed functions searchable and easy to map back to the original DOL addresses.

Used descriptive names only where partial behavior was evident, while retaining Unk in names such as Fighter_UnkSetFlag_8006CFBC, Fighter_UnkRecursiveFunc_8006D044, and Fighter_UnkProcessShieldHit_8006D1EC when semantics were not fully proven.
Updated C prototypes, C callsites, assembly branch labels, and assembly function pointer references together, avoiding mixed-symbol link or search drift after renaming.
Improved prototypes to match actual calling convention/readability, especially the damage wrapper taking Fighter* plus f32 damage rather than a vague void* and reversed-looking argument names.
Converted user_data handling to Fighter* in transformation/matrix code, which made field accesses more accurate and reduced misleading HSD_JObj-based local naming.
Replaced magic ground/air values with GA_Ground and GA_Air in touched code, improving future pattern recognition for fighter state logic.
Removed stale decomp.me scratch comments and 'not quite matched' style notes from cleaned-up functions where the diff no longer needed them, reducing noise in mature decompiled code.
decomp_lessons: When renaming a core symbol used by both C and assembly, update all assembly bl targets and @ha/@l address loads in the same PR; otherwise the codebase becomes hard to grep and may fail to link.
Address-suffixed descriptive names are useful in decomp work: they communicate current understanding while preserving the original address identity for cross-references.
Use Unk in a name when the behavior is only partially understood rather than overstating semantics; this PR uses names like Fighter_UnkTakeDamage_8006CC30 and Fighter_UnkApplyTransformation_8006C0F0.
Improving local variable names can reveal and document struct understanding without changing behavior, especially around fighterObj->user_data, callback fields, matrices, and camera offset vectors.
Replacing raw numeric state checks with enums such as GA_Ground and GA_Air is a low-risk cleanup that makes future matching and review easier.
Prototype cleanup matters even if the generated code is unchanged: correct types and argument names help future decompilers avoid misleading casts and wrong argument-order assumptions.
Do not force a rename for symbols that still live in another asm file or lack enough evidence; leaving func_8006DABC with a note is safer than inventing a misleading Fighter_* name.
naming_conventions: Fighter subsystem functions should use the Fighter_ prefix rather than generic func_ addresses when ownership is known.
Keep the original address suffix, e.g. Fighter_8006CDA4 and Fighter_UnkSetFlag_8006CFBC, to preserve searchability and address provenance.
Use Fighter_Unk... when the function's broad behavior is suspected but not fully known.
Use action-oriented names when callback purpose is visible, e.g. Fighter_UnkProcessGrab_8006CA5C, Fighter_UnkProcessShieldHit_8006D1EC, and Fighter_UnkCallCameraCallback_8006D9EC.
Callback helper naming in this PR includes Fighter_CallAcessoryCallbacks_8006C624; note the exact spelling 'Acessory' appears in the diff and should be searched as written.
Prefer typed local names such as fighter, cam_offset, fighter_mtx, and jobj_mtx over register-derived or misleading temporary names.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Assembly-only files were updated by changing symbol operands while preserving instruction bytes/comments, e.g. bl func_8006CFBC became bl Fighter_UnkSetFlag_8006CFBC with the same branch encoding.

Function pointer address construction in assembly was renamed consistently, e.g. func_8006C27C@ha/@l to Fighter_8006C27C@ha/@l, func_8006C80C to Fighter_8006C80C, and func_8006D9AC to Fighter_8006D9AC.
For renamed C functions used by undecompiled assembly, the key tactic was symbol synchronization rather than logic changes.
The cleanup retained address suffixes so future matching work can correlate C functions, assembly labels, and decomp.me scratches even after descriptive renaming.
Boolean simplifications and enum substitutions were applied carefully in decompiled C in ways that should preserve equivalent control flow while making source intent clearer.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename remaining func_8006DABC or move it out of ftanim.s.
Search for Fighter_CallAcessoryCallbacks_8006C624 spelling to see whether it was later corrected or kept for consistency.
Look for later cleanup of Fighter_UnkTakeDamage_8006CC30 to determine whether its exact semantics were confirmed.
Search subsequent PRs for Fighter_UnkRecursiveFunc_8006D044 and Fighter_8006D10C to understand linked-fighter or follower recursion behavior.
Check whether Master Hand, Crazy Hand, and Ice Climbers assembly callsites were later decompiled after these symbol renames.
